name: Lesser Key Solomon
description: The Lesser Key of Solomon (Lemegeton Clavicula Salomonis) is a pseudepigraphical grimoire focusing on demonology. It claims to be derived from King Solomon and outlines methods for summoning and controlling spirits. The grimoire is divided into five books: Ars Goetia (dealing with 72 demons), Ars Theurgia-Goetia (dealing with spirits of the cardinal directions), Ars Paulina (dealing with angels of the hours of the day and night), Ars Almadel (dealing with wax tablets used for divination), and Ars Notoria (purporting to grant knowledge and improve memory). Proponents believe the rituals described allow for communication and control over powerful entities, granting the practitioner knowledge, power, and assistance. Evidence cited includes the antiquity of the manuscripts, the complexity of the rituals, and anecdotal accounts of successful invocations. Mainstream scholarship views the Lesser Key as a product of the European Renaissance, drawing heavily on earlier grimoires, folklore, and religious traditions. Scholars argue that the grimoire reflects the anxieties and intellectual currents of its time, rather than representing genuine magical practices originating from King Solomon. The attribution to Solomon is considered a common trope used to lend authority to magical texts. The efficacy of the rituals is dismissed as either placebo effect, psychological projection, or delusion. The texts are analyzed for their literary, historical, and sociological significance rather than their supernatural claims.
subtitle: Grimoire of Demonology and Spirit Evocation

claims.claim: The rituals can summon and control demons.
claims.evidence: Ancient manuscripts detailing the rituals and anecdotal accounts of successful invocations.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence, reliance on subjective experiences, and psychological explanations for perceived effects.

claims.claim: The grimoire originates from King Solomon.
claims.evidence: Attribution within the text itself and purported connections to Solomonic magic.
claims.criticism: Pseudepigraphical nature of the text and lack of historical evidence linking it to Solomon.

claims.claim: The Ars Notoria grants knowledge and improves memory.
claims.evidence: Promises made within the text itself and testimonials from practitioners.
claims.criticism: Lack of scientific evidence and potential for placebo effect.

keyFigures.name: S.L. MacGregor Mathers
keyFigures.role: Translator and editor of the Ars Goetia.

keyFigures.name: Aleister Crowley
keyFigures.role: Prominent occultist who incorporated elements of the Lesser Key into his system of magic.

keyFigures.name: Owen Davies
keyFigures.role: Historian of magic and author of books on grimoires and witchcraft.

timeline.event: Development of early grimoires that influenced the Lesser Key.
Compilation of the Lesser Key of Solomon in its current form.
Translation and popularization of the Lesser Key by occultists like Mathers and Crowley.
